title: Cat Stiff Gait After Waking Up: Is It Arthritis?
meta_desc: Discover why your cat might be stiff after sleeping and learn how to help them. Understand urgency levels and why a video can assist your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Stiffness after waking, often called 'warm-up' lameness, is a common sign of osteoarthritis in cats and typically requires a routine veterinary visit rather than emergency care. To ensure an accurate diagnosis, capture a video of your cat’s movement at home to show your veterinarian.

What causes my cat to have a stiff gait when waking up?

It can be worrying to watch your cat struggle to stand or walk smoothly after a long nap. This stiffness, often referred to as 'warm-up' lameness, occurs when joints are stiff at rest but become more fluid once the cat starts moving. In many feline patients, this is a hallmark sign of osteoarthritis or age-related joint wear and tear.

Is my cat's stiffness after sleeping considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Monitor your cat's behavior for signs of chronic discomfort.
  • Understand that this is a low-urgency issue and not an immediate life-threatening crisis.
  • Book a routine veterinary consultation to discuss long-term comfort, weight management, and mobility aids.

How does taking a video of my cat's movement help with diagnosis?

  • Capture a video of your cat waking up and attempting to walk at home to show your vet, as cats often hide pain during clinic visits.
  • Use this footage as an invaluable triage tool to bypass the masking effects of adrenaline.
  • Allow your veterinarian to see the exact nature and location of the stiffness, leading to a much more accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Osteoarthritis is a common cause of persistent pain in cats and can manifest as altered joint function. Clinical signs of arthritis vary depending on the location and severity of the disease but may include lameness or decreased activity. In acute or active cases, synovial effusion and surrounding tissue swelling may be present, with manipulation of the joint causing pain; subtle cases may require flexion tests to elicit lameness. Chronic disease leads to a reduced range of motion due to fibrous thickening of the joint capsule. Diagnosis relies on clinical signs, physical examination, and imaging such as radiographs, which may reveal narrowing of the joint space and sclerosis of the subchondral bone.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1912)

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they aren't meowing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cats are stoic animals and rarely vocalize chronic pain. Stiffness, reluctance to jump, and sleeping more are their ways of showing discomfort.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human ibuprofen for stiffness?
faqs.answer: No. Never give cats human pain medication like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, as these are highly toxic and can be fatal to felines.

faqs.question: What can the vet do for a stiff gait?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians can provide feline-specific anti-inflammatory medications, joint supplements, or environmental recommendations to make your cat's life more comfortable.
